segments.text: One of the things I always noticed in a department when I was running it, anytime you're talking
about changes and suggestions from faculty, they pretend they're coming up with a big
original idea about how to do graduate program or how to do this, how to do that.
But when you investigate it in detail, you'll find that what they're proposing to do is
exactly the same way they did it where they came from.
And so I think anytime you get these outsiders coming in, you tend to, you do get some of
that mixture, but I'm not sure it's always that great.
Sometimes what we had was better than what they're trying to propose.
I'll tell you one other thing that I've noticed that I think A&M ought to improve on.
That is the way they treat their former presidents and so on.
Now, when I came here, Walton had been president, and I think he was the one that got us that
permanent fund, wasn't he?
Well, we began to get a better share.
I meant that was when he got, he was the one that looked up the law and got all that going.
And he was running the post office or something.
Then when Gilchrist stepped down, Fred Benson finally gave him an office over there in Zachary.
Then when Benson finally stepped down, old Flipsy wouldn't even give him an office anywhere for a long time.
And I just think that they lose a lot by not having these people more involved in.
Now, I went to this little junior college, this little Baptist junior college, and we had, I think we had chapel every morning.
And the guy that I took college algebra and trigonometry was born in about mid-1800s.
Dr. R.L.
Moore.
Big, tall, skinny guy, wore a frock-tail coat and wing-tip collars with a string tie.
And he didn't have any problem with what was right and wrong.
But he was very revered.
He sat up on the stage every day, and the president always deferred to him.
And he was still teaching a course in college algebra and trigonometry, and he taught one in Greek.
That was his, but he wanted to get associated with the average student.
Not just the preachers.
And every morning, I'll tell you this one, you might enjoy it.
Every morning in class, he would tell us a little moral story.
And it was also back in those depression days of encouraging students.
Now, I don't know whether we do enough of that.
Motivating them, just telling them, you've got to make something of yourself.
And he mentioned that Dick Wayne, that Hall that used to be in mathematics, was in one of his classes.
And he really worked hard.
And he went on to be professor at one of the greater universities, is what he said.
But he was, of course, against smoking and everything.
He was so against smoking that what he did, they said at one time, was buy up all of the tobacco in the little town there,
take it out on the middle of the campus, and burn it.
But anyhow, in those years, and that was about 1939, the number one hit parade song was,
I don't want to set the world on fire, I just want to start a flame in your heart.
And old Dr. Moore came in one morning, and he said, and he always called us young people.
He said, young people, I just don't know what this world's coming to.
He said, I was coming to walk, walking to work this morning, and I passed this young man.
He was pushing this wheelbarrow, and he was singing, I don't want to set the world on fire.
And young people, he was.
He wasn't.
He wasn't.
So, but I, now there is an example.
We're not getting, I think there's a tendency in all organizations to let us just bury the guy once we get through with him.
Instead of, if he was good enough to serve and run your organization, he would really be good enough to have around to help you.
And we need to, that's one of the things that we need to take advantage of.
I propose to.
I propose to President Bowen, and I couldn't think of a better title than alumni professor.
And I said, every year when the various faculty members retire, why not then try to look and say,
which of these would we like to be able to still claim were part of our team, still use their talents and abilities,
and ask the products of that department or that unit,
would you help us?
And I said, why not raise the money to give this person a small little honorarium
so that this person could still teach a course or do a little research.
We could call on for advice.
Randy Mattson, then executive director of the former students, he said, I could raise that money with a few phone calls.
But Dr. Bowen didn't see that as a good idea.
I wonder how he will feel when he retires.
Because see, just look what's happened to them.
Mm-hmm.
They're just walking around.
Nobody.
Yep.
Yep.
And Fred, old Flipsy, wouldn't even, and then Flipsy would just browbeat the hell out of you.
I'll give you one story that got him off of my back.
I was up there meeting with him and Skip Fletcher.
Boy, they just beat me all over the place about this and that, getting more money for this and more money for that, and a lot of things.
And so I just kind of let it slide off.

When I got back to the office, I decided the way I'll fix him is I'm going to write him a letter, and I'm going to tell him, you told me several things this morning in our discussion, and before I tell my faculty what you said, I want to make sure that I got the details correct.
Then I listed them.
I didn't hear anything from old Flipsy.
And then I throw him in and he says, Charlie.
we're going to make peace between your house and my house and he never did uh jump me anymore
now he succeeded uh dean benson no he was assistant to richardson oh and uh he and i got
along fine after that but he didn't try to come after me anymore he would he would intimidate
people if he knew that he could you know there's some people who run over others if they realize
they can yes and so that really cooled his heels and from then on even richardson would get him to
go talk to me rather than do it himself after i told him that if they wanted the job they could
have it but richardson helped me a lot we worked together but i just uh you know stood my ground
on that i didn't see any use of my stepping down at that particular time trace for for this uh
recording after fred benson
you served as dean who were the other deans uh let's see page was the next dean that was selected
describe him and uh well he was a very nice individual where'd he come from uh let's see
i can't remember as a northern university he was in kind of an impossible situation skip fletcher
had the tease if you have one man in charge of the texas engineering experiment station other in
charge of the academic
it'll never work because i started out working under that when barlow and mellow had it and they
they didn't even talk to one another they sent what they sent letters up and down the hall on that
new engineering building and each one of them expects you to work full-time for them is what
the problem is and so paige had it all against him to begin with but he didn't really have any guts
and he he wouldn't go after i don't think he went after money like he should
have for for us because uh our operating budgets were so small that i used most all my gift and grant
funds to do things that i should have been spending state money on all travel i i i paid
for it with gift and grant go give papers i paid for uh paid for that with gift and grant money
and i couldn't get him to he wouldn't stand up for you over there and uh so
uh that was uh i think he's a nice individual pleasant but uh he just didn't have the grit
to stand up to somebody to tell him you know we need this who was next after him um i guess they
had to have several interims in there mcdonald was an interim for a while and uh i guess that
was about the next one i guess the next one was uh was richardson describe him where he came from
uh he came from mit and i think he's a real good manager he doesn't like any confrontation at all
he can hardly but he's really good at uh one-to-one discussions and uh but he didn't didn't
like to to have anybody uh criticize you know a lot of times things really ought to be debated
even if people get a little hot to find out where what the real facts are get them out and open what
the things that people need and what they really want uh and he didn't like us he didn't like he's
just individual it seems to be kind of timid he's not a good speaker uh he did all right running
i think he did a good job except he was just a little timid on some of those things
and he had flipsy doing all of the uh nasty work he had him doing it for him
uh but i think he he was good and he was also good in uh when he got into the higher position but he
representation of Texas A&M University.
You need a man that can get up there and
speak, and he couldn't do it.
Describe to someone who did not, who might not be
aware of the realities of the College of Engineering, Texas Engineering
Experiment Station, and the Texas Engineering Extension Service.
Explain
how those are related, and describe their functions.
All right the Texas A&M,
the College of Engineering is a part of Texas A&M University.
The Texas
Engineering Experiment Station is a separate state agency on a chart.
It
would occupy a position parallel to Texas A&M University, and also as I
recall Texas Extension Service is the same way.
And I'm not sure where the TTI
is.
as it may be where do they report they report they report now to the they well
they've had them report in different ones they report now to the Dean Dean of
Engineering because the Dean is in charge of all of it one time they
reported to different people and there's where all the problem because the poor
little faculty member he has to work for both of them all three of them and so
all the problems come down and he's the stretch and you as a department head
needed to be able I had to work with all of them yeah and you might want to get
funding from all of them I'll tell you one another thing that I think that
universities don't work nearly hard enough at in fact sometimes they go the
wrong way well there are two things one of them just remind me if I don't come
back to it is the coursework and another one is on
funding funding people.
The one on funding, all the time I was head of the department, I was able
to keep everybody on a 12-month basis, regardless of whether they had research or not.
And we
did that by using gift and grant funds, and I just juggled it to where we made it work
because it's very difficult to live on a nine-month salary.
And so I felt like everybody that's
around the university ought to be full-time employed.
And I think that one of the things
that stirs up more resentment on a university campus is having professors on a nine-month
basis and administrators whom they are not really overenthusiastic about to begin with,
any administrator, on a 12-month basis.
And here these people are trying to get by on
a nine-month basis.
And I think that what we ought to do is we've got a big,
plant, millions of dollars invested in the facilities here.
We ought to expand the summer
program to where we've got 12-month employment throughout the year.
We're not an agricultural
society anymore.
That was the reason that was originally set up.
We ought to get around
to the fact that we are a 12-month operation and that we need full-time employees.
And
I think you'd get a lot more loyalty among employees if you had because these can't get
it and it's very difficult in some areas.
It's very difficult in some areas to get research during the summer.
And this shouldn't
be the way we operate.
And we should try to do it on a 12-month basis and have people
doing something.
There ought to be a, and I think there's a lot of students that want
to take courses, more courses in the summer and can't get them because the money's not
allocated for summer courses.
They're trying, seem to be trying to keep those down.
But
they ought to be going the other way, is try to get more summer courses.
Jumping way back.
Yeah.
Back now, as in those 23 years that you were department head, looking back, who were some
of the key faculty members that you brought in?
I brought nearly the whole bunch.
I know, but who were the ones that made the most, I'm not going to ask you the negative,
just the positive.
Well, actually most of them did all right.
Who were the ones that were the cream of the crop?
Well, Eubank was a good one.
Who was?
Harvey was a good one.
Describe each one of them as you're going along.
Okay.
Okay.
So, he came to us from Rose Poly as an undergraduate and went to, I've forgotten where he did go
for his graduate work.
He was in thermodynamics, and he helped get the thermodynamics program
started.
And he did, he was a good methodical teacher.
He did teaching as well as research.
And he's still involved.
And then another one that I brought in was Ron Darby and he
may not be. He wasn't the only one. He was a good teacher. But he was a good teacher.
He wasn't as successful in getting research, he was probably more negative on putting in
for it, saying he wouldn't get it or something like that, but he was a real good individual.
So that was another one.
Dick Davidson was another real good one that we brought in, and he had been good throughout
his whole career.
He was an excellent teacher, and original ideas, and good generally across the board.
Let's see, Ray Anthony was another one, I brought him in.
As you were recruiting these people, how did you identify the high quality faculty
members, the potential faculty members, how did you find them?
Oh.
Well, some of these we checked to find out who was coming out of Rice.
I knew the profs down there, the good ones, and I got some from them, and Ray, we'd had
as a graduate student, went over to Texas, got his Ph.D., so we knew him.
We also hired Hall in later, and although he's been through a lot of the things, I don't
think he measures up to these other people.
But he was, he's a really go-getter as far as hunting funds, but he doesn't have the
staying power that I'd say some of the others did, as far as time after time.
Let's see, I guess that, those are the old stand-bys that are over there.
What were some, some kind of big step, big achievement research projects that the department
did?
Well, I think that the, they did, made numerous contributions in, in thermodynamics.
They had a thermodynamics group that got together, and they all had several grants going there.
And that carried on for a good long while to where it's gotten now very difficult to
get that type of money.
They want to put money on the ground.
They want to put money on something that's going to be, turn the world around, you know.
But this was collecting good, solid thermodynamic data that they did.
Also in catalysis, there was quite a bit, we made a lot of progress in catalysis and
kinetics.
Ray Anthony and others worked on that.
And Ron did some work on rheology.
And I did a lot of work on distillation.
In fact, I've got two papers.
I'm trying to get out of the field, but I've got two papers right now that are going to
be published.
And one of them is going into a journal where one of the editors are retiring, and they
asked me to write a paper.
And I did, and I used a concept I've been using in teaching a class.
And I wrote it up, and sure enough, it's accepted.
It really wasn't any experimental research.
It was just concept.
It was ability, technique for representation of n-dimensional space.
And not just three, four, five in two-dimensional space.
And represent all the complexities there in two dimension.
And it's real simple.
It's very similar to just representing it for two components.
And then another one that I've got coming out, I didn't really think about getting a
paper.
A guy from Vladivostok, a Russian, wrote me on email that he'd like me to send him some
of the papers.
That was a paper.
That we had.
And instead of doing that, what I did was just sit down and I said, well, that poor
guy doesn't have any money, can't even buy the papers, and so on.
What I'll do is just sit down and derive him some equations and give him ones that we haven't
ever published before.
And so I did.
And he's ended up, he took those and made the problem a little bigger.
And he's got a paper written now that I'm going to be a publisher with him.
From Vladivostok.
It's on the eastern front over there.
Who were the big, were there any key industry supporters for chemical engineering research
here during your years?
Oh, yeah.
We had Dow continue to support us some, and there weren't no real big ones, but enough
grants.
Dow, I got $20,000 a year to help me do this numerical stuff and theoretical developments,
which was a good, back in those days, that's a good little sum of money just to have coming
in every year.
So we had some money like that.
We had a lot.
I also had some from the National Science Foundation.
We got quite a bit that way, and other organizations.
Also Amoco helped quite a bit with our labs and so forth.
So we managed one way or another to get grants from one another and the government and all
these people.
So we had work done there.
We had a great deal of management.
We got to the center of it.
A lot of our general leadership, our field management was under the leadership of the
National Science Foundation.
They were very important in the medical and technical development of chemical engineering.
They were there for the board of directors.
And that was going on.
They were there for the research, and so they were there for the research.
They were going to keep enough coming in to keep the research program going and pay the
graduate students as well.
Under your leadership, which do you think was more important?
The theoretical or the practical research projects?
Oh, I don't know that it was all that much.
There really can't be divided that well down the line because there was a lot of theoretical
concepts involved in a lot of them.
There can be practical problems.
And I don't know that I'd want to make a judgment on that.
Of these key faculty, in your years of leadership,
did we lose some key faculty members to industry or another institution?
No, we really didn't lose many people.
The only one, we lost one or two, but we weren't concerned about it
because we weren't sure we wanted them anyway,
and so they got hired somewhere else.
We brought a whole group of young ones.
Watson is another one that's done real well over there I didn't mention.
But what we did was bring in these young ones,
and we really rode hard on them to make sure they got started in research.
How did you do that?
Well, we met with them to find out what they were doing
and give them information.
Well, we met with them to find out what they were doing
and giving them advice and also putting a little pressure on them.
You want to try to write this proposal or that one?
And so I think they thought we might have been a little hard on them,
but there's the time to do something is when you first get them.
And so they made it, and I got old Watson.
Actually, I told Richardson this, and he was amazed that I did it.
Watson came up for tenure,
and the full professors looked at him and decided they didn't want to give him
tenure.
And so they reported me, and I said,
well, I'm not really sure whether they know this man well enough.
I'm going to see what.
So I appointed another committee made up of the associate professors
of his precise peers to make a judgment on him,
what they thought about him.
And they came up positive.
And so I decided I'd go ahead and recommend him for tenure.
And old Richardson says, you mean you went against your full professors?
I said, well, I sure did, because I thought he deserved it.
I don't know whether, I guess it could be debatable yet,
but he's doing real well.
Now, as an outsider, we heard a way over in history,
I heard that it was common practice in engineering in those years
that a new faculty member got a little bit of funding
through the experiment station or some research account to start with.
Yeah, we did some of that, and I gave them some department money.
And that they were told that by the time three, four, five years in,
in the future, they were expected to be getting their own research.
Well, we tried to get them as soon as possible to start looking for it.
Okay.
Because it takes two or three years after you even start.
So we had them start looking for it in some area that they were familiar with.
And then we gave them enough to,
tried to give them enough to get started trying to do something.
Were there some individual honors that came to the department
during your years of leadership that you'd like to?
Well, I don't remember.
All of them.
We got nearly every one of them as these distinguished awards.
Nearly everybody in the department got teaching or research.
What about national or international awards?
Yeah, there are several of those.
As the years went on, with what other schools,
by the time you retired as department head,
what other schools should we be compared with in chemical engineering?
Oh, I think that,
University of Texas, Rice, all of these are strong schools.
Were we in there?
Well, we, I don't know that we're,
University of Texas always had a little more support than we ever had.
Boy, I'll tell you, we had so little support in the 50s.
I remember Norman Road asked, they were building everything up at,
what was the name of this school that went over to the university system?
Arlington.
They were building all kinds of engineering buildings up there.
And we still had those old buildings over there where electrical was, you know.
Back, I was in the Navy unit with Norman Road and Kenny Hancock and Fred Benson and Schiller.
And Norman told us, he says, you know, I was talking to,
I guess it was Mr. Gilchrist.
I was talking to Mr. Gilchrist yesterday.
I said, Mr. Gilchrist, you know how Norman was.
He's very outspoken.
You remember him, don't you?
Norman Road.
Are we getting ready to move?
The college engineering up to Arlington?
Mr. Gilchrist goes, I don't know why.
He said, well, you're putting all the money in buildings up there instead of down here.
But those were really lean years.
I'll tell you, they really were.
By comparison, what were the early 80s like?
Well, where the change was was when we got Zachary.
And that was about 73 or somewhere along there.
And that, then we really were beginning to be,
able to compete.
Actually, when we had those old facilities,
I didn't even like to take former graduate,
I mean, potential graduate students through a lab
because it was so ratty.
In fact, when I had old Charlie over here,
Jones that was president of Exxon,
and Jim Galloway,
I didn't realize they were as bad as they were.
And I took them through,
and we sat back down up there into my office,
and old Charlie Jones says,
Chuck, I'm clear you need some more facilities.
And so an outsider could see it.
And we didn't have, we got, we were on the list,
and that used to worry Doc all the time,
getting a new building.
And I decided that's one thing I'm not going to worry about.
It's more important what you do,
regardless of what the dang building looks like,
than having the building,
because it's really the states anyway.
But we were always first on the list,
and then when we'd come along,
Petroleum would get one.
In fact, they got two before we ever got one.
And, and,
and, and,
and, and,
and, and,
and, and,
and, and,
and, and,
and, and,
and, and,
and, and,
and, and,
and, and,
and, and,
and, and,
and, and,
and, and,
but finally we got in the loop and got some facilities,
but we really didn't have that kind of support for a long time.
And so we got behind and also not having girls over here.
It was hard to draw graduate students in all those formative years,
and so it wasn't an easy battle.
As the years went on in the late 70s and 80s,
did we begin to be competitive in salaries?
Yeah, we're,
we're closer to being competitive now.
I think we probably are now.
No, after they started kicking them up,
we became, we were,
started being competitive with salaries.
I don't know exactly how they are.
Some of these people watch them and plot them all the time.
I don't, but I think our salary is pretty good right now.
For you as a department head who also was a real world faculty member,
what were the greatest joys of all these years?
Well, I guess,
actually, and it still is,
is really getting up and teaching in class.
Interaction with students and a lot of things I'm teaching,
the courses I'm taught is I develop the books that I'm teaching out of
and develop the theory that I'm teaching.
So it's just a real pleasure to get up there and be able to explain things to students.
I did, I taught a class this morning at eight o'clock.
Please share if you can any examples of Aggie,
good bull, tricks,
pranks, that you may have been aware of.
Surely, students must have tried to pull something on a chemical engineering faculty member somewhere along the line.
Oh, well,
I can tell you a couple that happened to faculty members,
one of them.
I had this graduate student,
Nathan Welch, who was,
I think he was hyperactive.
And he, you'd see him walking up and down the hall.
He was sharp as a tack.
And, he'd have a Coke in one hand and a cigarette in the other and a pencil over his ear.
And, just walking as fast as he could.
And so one day I had to go out of town and I asked him to teach my class.
And, I said,
now, I want you to really give them a good presentation.
And, he said,
well, I'll give them one they'll never forget.
So, when I got back,
what had happened was,
the students told me,
that old Nate got in there,
and he was just going like a house on fire.
He was taking a swig of that Coke,
and then he'd smoke a cigarette,
and then he'd put the pencil up on his ear,
and then he'd smoke a cigarette again.
Finally, he laid the cigarette up on his ear.
And, he says,
don't you all tell Dr. Holland this,
or this will be my last lecture.
And then, old Dr. Lindsey,
I remember one time,
he was always huffing and puffing around.
I was teaching a course over there in the new engineering building.
He liked to go on inspection trips.
We were getting ready to go on an inspection trip.
And so, Doc was preparing them.
And, he came over.
And, he came blustering in there,
and he told them about the trip.
